Innovations in Carbon Removal and Storage
=========================================

*   [Climate Tech 101](https://netzeroinsights.com/resources/tag/climate-tech-101/)
*   [🌪️ GHG CRS](https://netzeroinsights.com/resources/category/ghg-capture-removal-and-storage/)

*   [April 14, 2025](https://netzeroinsights.com/resources/2025/04/14/)

Carbon dioxide (CO₂) makes up just **[0.04% of the atmosphere](https://science.nasa.gov/earth/climate-change/greenhouse-gases/the-atmosphere-getting-a-handle-on-carbon-dioxide/)** but any slight variation in its atmospheric concentration can have profound consequences. Since the Industrial Age, anthropogenic activities have doubled CO₂ levels from 280 ppm to 412 parts per million (ppm) today leading to extreme weather events. Scientific research confirms that human-induced emissions have led to a global temperature increase of at least **[1.1°C since 1880](https://earthobservatory.nasa.gov/world-of-change/global-temperatures)**.

To tackle the climate crisis, Climate Tech is advancing solutions in carbon capture, removal, and storage. Carbon Dioxide Removal (CDR) extracts CO₂ directly from the atmosphere and stores it in geological formations, oceans, or products. In contrast, Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) captures CO₂ from industrial emissions at the source—such as cement, steel, and chemical plants—before locking it away permanently underground.

While some commercial projects are already operational, many of these technologies are still in early deployment stages. Strategic investment and policy support will be crucial to scale these solutions and accelerate decarbonization.

Investment in **[GHG capture, removal, and storage](https://stateofclimatetech.com/)** is growing, rising from 0.7% of Climate Tech funding in 2020 to 3.5% in 2024. An estimated **[$196 billion in investment](https://www.woodmac.com/news/opinion/ccus-196-billion-investment-opportunity/)** is needed over the next decade to develop global carbon capture, utilization, and storage (CCUS) infrastructure.

**Solutions across Carbon Removal, Capture and Storage**
--------------------------------------------------------

Reaching Net Zero isn’t just about reducing emissions—it requires actively pulling CO₂ from the atmosphere to counter decades of accumulation. The **[Climate Tech Taxonomy](https://netzeroinsights.com/resources/the-climate-tech-taxonomy/)**offers a structured framework for mapping solutions that are reversing global warming.

![Image 3: Market Maps - Carbon Removal, Capture and Storage](https://i0.wp.com/netzeroinsights.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Market-Maps-Carbon-Removal-Capture-and-Storage.png?resize=500%2C500&ssl=1)

Market Map illustrating companies and key data points across GHG capture, removal, and storage as of March 2025. *Companies/deals may exist across multiple stages of the value chain.

### **Carbon Capture**

Hard-to-abate industries such as cement, steel, iron, and chemicals are among the largest sources of global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, collectively accounting for **[40% of total emissions](https://www.weforum.org/press/2024/12/emissions-fall-in-hard-to-abate-sectors-but-still-off-track-to-reach-2050-net-zero-targets/#:~:text=Indeed%2C%20five%20out%20of%20the,efficiency%20and%20more%20recycled%20metals.)**. In 2024, global carbon emissions hit an all-time high, with CO₂ emissions reaching a staggering **[41.6 gigatonnes](https://www.carbonbrief.org/analysis-global-co2-emissions-will-reach-new-high-in-2024-despite-slower-growth/)**.

Carbon capture technology plays a pivotal role in mitigating the impact of these emissions by capturing carbon dioxide (CO₂) directly from industrial processes before it reaches the atmosphere. Innovations in point-source carbon capture enable large-scale decarbonization by offering a viable pathway to reduce emissions from these critical sectors.

Over the past decade, advancements have been made in the development of both engineered and nature-based solutions for carbon removal. Technologies such as soil carbon sequestration, ocean alkalinity enhancement, rock weathering, and direct air capture are being deployed alongside nature-based approaches like afforestation and coastal wetland restoration.

##### **Notable startups to watch out in Carbon Capture**

[**Heirloom**](https://www.heirloomcarbon.com/)’s direct air capture (DAC) technology enhances limestone’s natural ability to absorb CO₂ from the air. The spent limestone is continuously recycled through the system, lowering costs and reducing the need for extensive mining. In December 2024, Heirloom raised $150M in Series B funding from Breakthrough Energy Ventures, Lowercarbon Capital, Mitsui & Co., and others.

[**Terradot**](https://terradot.earth/) scales carbon removal through enhanced rock weathering (ERW) by applying basalt to farmland, where it reacts with rainwater to permanently sequester CO₂. Their platform enables precise measurement, reporting, and verification (MRV) of captured carbon while improving soil health. In December 2024, Terradot secured $54M in Series A funding from Acre Venture Partners, Sheryl Sandberg, Cisco, Microsoft, and others.

[**CarbonQuest**](https://carbonquest.com/) delivers modular, on-site carbon capture solutions for natural gas-based emissions from CHP systems, fuel cells, and boilers. The captured CO₂ is liquefied and sold for use in concrete manufacturing, jet fuel, and chemical processes. In February 2025, CarbonQuest raised $20M in Series A funding from Riverbend Energy Group, Energy Capital Ventures, and Aligned Climate Capital.

### **Carbon Conversion and Utilisation**

Carbon conversion and utilization technologies repurpose captured carbon dioxide (CO₂) from industrial emissions. The captured CO₂ is used in urea production, cement, and carbonation of beverages and more. Lately, new applications such as CO₂-based synthetic fuels, chemicals, and building aggregates are gaining traction.

##### **Carbon Conversion and Utilisation startups that should be on your radar**

[**Twelve**](https://www.twelve.co/)’s industrial-scale electrochemical technology uses CO₂ electrolysis to convert CO₂ and water using renewable electricity into jet fuel, apparel, sportswear, and other products. This zero-emission process eliminates the need for fossil fuel-based feedstocks. Twelve raised $83M in Series C funding in February 2025 from Mitsui & Co., Development Bank of Japan, The Climate Pledge, and others.

Dutch startup [**Paebbl**](https://paebbl.com/) transforms CO₂ into sustainable building materials through carbon mineralization, permanently storing up to 300 kg of CO₂ per ton of material. Paebbl raised $25M in Series A funding in October 2024 from Amazon, Pale Blue Dot, Capnamic, 2050, and Holcim MAQER Ventures.

### **GHG Removal Real-Time Monitoring**

Monitoring, reporting, and verification (MRV) are critical to the success of GHG removal efforts since they ensure accurate accounting of captured emissions. Real-time GHG monitoring plays a crucial role in tracking CO₂ and other emissions removed from the atmosphere through solutions like carbon capture and storage and direct air capture (DAC). Key technologies in this space include data management and analytics, CO₂ leak detection, and carbon sequestration monitoring.

##### **Notable startup to watch out in GHG Removal Real-Time Monitoring**

French company [**Geolinks**](https://geolinks-services.com/) offers sustainable geophysical monitoring solutions using ambient-noise based technology for mining, CO2 geological storage, geothermal energy, and other industries. Geolinks raised $ 1.6M in April 2024 from EIT InnoEnergy.

**Carbon Removal and Storage Gains Global Momentum**
----------------------------------------------------

Climate Tech is advancing the efficiency and scalability of GHG capture, removal, and storage. These solutions also drive carbon utilization, create economic opportunities through carbon markets, and support net-zero goals by balancing unavoidable emissions.

To accelerate adoption, investors need a clear view of where capital is flowing and which technologies are gaining traction. With comprehensive market intelligence, businesses and investors can identify high-impact opportunities to navigate this rapidly evolving sector, and strategically position themselves in the net-zero transition.

SUMMARY:
The International Energy Agency or IEA emphasizes the importance of addressing this severe situation, recognizing Carbon Capture, Utilization and Storage (CCUS) as a key component in the fight against climate change (Gunderson et al., 2020). Globally, carbon emissions from fossil fuel use and industrial activity have reached historic levels, considerably contributing to climate change (He et al., 2023). CO 2 can be captured from large sources, such as power plants, natural gas processing facilities and some industrial processes, using various technologies that separate CO 2 from the flue gas or process stream (Bains et al., 2017). Post-combustion capture is a technology that separates CO 2 from the exhaust gas after the fuel is combusted (Wang et al., 2017).

Abstract
--------

[Carbon capture and storage](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/carbon-capture-and-storage "Learn more about Carbon capture and storage from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") represented as [CCS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/carbon-capture-and-storage "Learn more about CCS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), is a technique that can be used to cut down on emissions of CO 2 from industrial sources. These mechanisms can balance the excess [fossil fuel](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/fossil-fuel "Learn more about fossil fuel from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") usage and lead to effective carbon capture from the atmosphere and storing it in safe spaces. This can negate [global warming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/global-warming "Learn more about global warming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and send the carbon back to geological spaces inside the earth. This review covers the operational mechanism of such technologies from its inception to the material innovation along with the transport of CO 2 and its storage options. Breakthroughs in recent years have made it possible to design effective carbon capture and safe spaces for its storage. A comprehensive worldwide [case studies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/case-study "Learn more about case studies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are presented for both successful CCS project implementation and their [environmental impact assessment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-impact-assessment "Learn more about environmental impact assessment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Lessons learned from these case examples are reflected through the challenges and policy hurdles with its impact on the global economy. An outlook is provided for the role of CCS in net [zero emissions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/zero-emissions "Learn more about zero emissions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), renewables integration and advancing CCS research. By leveraging innovation across capture, utilization, and storage stages, CCS holds immense potential to play a transformative role in combating [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and achieving global sustainability goals.

1. Introduction
---------------

Existing air pollution levels reveal significant challenges that need to be addressed. While regulations have become more stringent to combat pollutants like SO 2, NO _x_, ozone, and fine particles, new challenges have emerged, such as the impact of [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on [air quality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/air-quality "Learn more about air quality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), leading to increased ground-level ozone and [particulate matter](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/atmospheric-aerosol "Learn more about particulate matter from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") levels globally ([Goren et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib120)). The continuous use of [fossil fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/fossil-fuel "Learn more about fossil fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the industrial sectors suggests the dire need for comprehensive reduction measures in utilizing fossil fuel-based energy sources ([Lau et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0054)). This contributes to the catastrophic increase in global [carbon emissions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/carbon-dioxide-emission "Learn more about carbon emissions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The International Energy Agency or IEA emphasizes the importance of addressing this severe situation, recognizing Carbon Capture, Utilization and Storage (CCUS) as a key component in the fight against [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Gunderson et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0037)). CCUS is the capture of CO 2 mostly from major [point sources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/source-point "Learn more about point sources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as power plants and industrial sites ([Thomas et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0102)). While recognizing the real-world consequences of such emissions, the gravity of the situation becomes further clear. For example, the ``Green-Roof Initiative'' in China demonstrates the consequences of unmanaged carbon emissions from power plants and industrial activity ([Dong et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0028)). [Afforestation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/afforestation "Learn more about Afforestation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), a natural process used by this program, demonstrates the scalable potential of using plants to sequester CO 2. Concurrently, industrial activities contribute significantly to carbon emissions, prompting immediate action.

In terms of CCUS, the mechanisms that have faced challenges include the high costs and lengthy timelines for deployment. Currently, there are only 42 operational commercial CCUS projects globally, which can capture and store just 0.13 % of the world's annual energy and industry-related CO 2 emissions. The high costs and lack of profitability for many CCUS projects have hindered their widespread deployment ([Chen et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib123)). Additionally, the availability of suitable [geological storage](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/geological-storage "Learn more about geological storage from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") sites for the captured CO 2 is limited, and transporting the CO 2 to these sites can be challenging and costly. CCUS plants are known to capture 10.5–10.8 % of CO 2 emissions while increasing air pollution and social costs ([Jacobson, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib124)). Environmental indices and CCUS offer a thorough examination of the global climate change situation, emphasizing the importance of regulating carbon emissions. Globally, carbon emissions from fossil fuel use and industrial activity have reached historic levels, considerably contributing to climate change ([He et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0039)). According to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), the Earth's warming trend is mostly due to higher concentrations of greenhouse gases, with [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") playing a key role ([Bhattacharya and Bose, 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0010)). To emphasize the gravity of the environmental crisis credible sources such as the [World Meteorological Organization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/world-meteorological-organization "Learn more about World Meteorological Organization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (WMO) have also expressed concerns over rising global temperatures, extreme weather events, and ecological disruptions ([Change, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0018)).

CCUS supports the achievement of the United Nations [Sustainable Development Goals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/sustainable-development-goals "Learn more about Sustainable Development Goals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or UN SDGs, especially SDG 13, which focuses on taking action to combat climate change. [CCS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/carbon-capture-and-storage "Learn more about CCS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is an essential technology that countries must utilise to fulfil their Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) as outlined in the [Paris Agreement](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/paris-agreement "Learn more about Paris Agreement from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Bhattacharya et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib121)). CCUS contributes to the attainment of Sustainable Development Goal 13 by effectively absorbing and sequestering CO 2 emissions originating from industrial sources, including chemical plants and [bioenergy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioenergy "Learn more about bioenergy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") plants, and securely storing them in geological formations. This action decreases the amount of greenhouse gases released into the atmosphere and aids in the effort to alleviate climate change. According to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), limiting [global warming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/global-warming "Learn more about global warming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to 1.5° Celsius which is the Paris Agreement's aspirational goal requires significant and rapid carbon emission reductions ([Masson-Delmotte et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0063)). In addressing this environmental imperative, the present review emphasizes the need for CCUS as a key component in reducing emissions. Recognizing the worldwide status of climate change, the analysis incorporates information from the International Energy Agency (IEA) and concludes that carbon capture technologies are critical for attaining the emission reductions required to reach climate targets.

### 1.1. Objectives of the study and its structure

This study evaluates improvements in CCUS technologies, focusing on the intricacies of CCUS foundations. Understanding the [CCUS process](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/storage-process "Learn more about CCUS process from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is critical, as it involves the collection of carbon dioxide emissions, particularly from big point sources such as power plants and industrial facilities. The paper dives into various carbon capture technologies to provide a thorough understanding of the various techniques used in CCUS. The exploration extends to the underlying principles, operational mechanisms, and materials associated with each technology. This includes an assessment of the latest [sorbents](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/sorbent "Learn more about sorbents from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), solvents, and membranes crucial for efficient carbon capture. The study recognizes the dynamic nature of CCUS advancements, acknowledging ongoing research and development efforts that shape the landscape with cutting-edge technologies.

2. Carbon capture, utilization and storage fundamentals
-------------------------------------------------------

[CO](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/carbon-monoxide "Learn more about CO from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")2 is one of the main greenhouse gases that contribute to [global warming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/global-warming "Learn more about global warming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). According to the International Energy Agency, fossil fuel-related [CO](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-monoxide "Learn more about CO from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")2 emissions reached a record high of 36.3 Gigatons in 2021, accounting for about 79 % of the total anthropogenic [CO](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/carbon-monoxide "Learn more about CO from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")2 ([He at al. 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0039)). About 50 % of the cumulative anthropogenic CO 2 emissions between 1750 and 2021 have been produced in the last 30 years, mainly from energy and industrial processes, such as [power generation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/power-generation "Learn more about power generation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), transport, and [cement production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/cement-production "Learn more about cement production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Dods et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0027)). CCUS is a process that aims to reduce the amount of CO 2 released into the atmosphere from fossil [fuel combustion](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/fuel-combustion "Learn more about fuel combustion from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and other industrial activities, and thereby mitigate [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Bhattacharya and Bose, 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0010)). CCUS can also mean the removal of CO 2 directly or indirectly from the atmosphere. The overview of CCUS is shown in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#fig0001).

Fig. 1. A compilation of techniques capable of [capturing carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/capturing-carbon-dioxide "Learn more about capturing carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CO2) emissions originating from significant point sources.

CO 2 can be captured from large sources, such as power plants, [natural gas processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/natural-gas-processing "Learn more about natural gas processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") facilities and some industrial processes, using various technologies that separate CO 2 from the [flue gas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/flue-gas "Learn more about flue gas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or process stream ([Bains et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0009)). Capture from the open atmosphere is also possible, using techniques that extract CO 2 from the ambient air. The captured CO 2 is then compressed and transported to a suitable deep, underground location, where it can be stored or buried in geological formations, such as [depleted oil](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/depleted-oil "Learn more about depleted oil from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and gas reservoirs, deep saline aquifers, or [coal seams](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/coal-seam "Learn more about coal seams from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The storage of CO 2 in these formations relies on various trapping mechanisms that prevent or delay the migration of CO 2 to the surface, such as structural, residual, solubility, and mineral trapping ([Zhang et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0119)).

### 2.1. Understanding the CCUS process

The first step of CCUS is to capture CO 2 from the [flue gas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/flue-gas "Learn more about flue gas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or process stream of a [point source](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/source-point "Learn more about point source from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as a coal-fired power plant, a [cement plant](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/cement-plant "Learn more about cement plant from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), a steel mill, or a [natural gas processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/natural-gas-processing "Learn more about natural gas processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") plant ([Bains et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0009)). The choice of CO 2 capture technology depends on the characteristics of the source and the desired purity and pressure of the CO 2 stream ([Porter et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0077)). The capture of CO 2 can be achieved through three main technologies: post-combustion capture, pre-combustion capture, and oxy-fuel capture. Post-combustion capture is a technology that separates CO 2 from the [exhaust gas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/exhaust-gas "Learn more about exhaust gas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") after the fuel is combusted ([Wang et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0110)).

A major challenge for this technology is the low concentration of CO 2 (4–14 % v/v) in the exhaust gas, which limits the efficiency and feasibility of CO 2 capture. The most common CO 2 separation process in post-combustion capture is absorption using an amine-based solvent. However, this process requires strong [chemical solvents](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/solvent "Learn more about chemical solvents from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and high energy consumption for solvent regeneration. The advantage of this process is that it can produce high-purity CO 2, which can be utilized in various applications, such as [enhanced oil recovery](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/enhanced-oil-recovery "Learn more about enhanced oil recovery from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), urea production, and the food or [beverage industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/beverage-industry "Learn more about beverage industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Liang et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0058)).

Pre-combustion capture systems are designed to separate CO 2 and hydrogen (H 2) from the [syngas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/synthesis-gas "Learn more about syngas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") stream produced by the [gasifier](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/gasifiers "Learn more about gasifier from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in integrated gasification combined cycle (IGCC) power plants ([Sheikh et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0089)). In this process, fuel is partially oxidized by oxygen and/or steam at high pressure and temperature to form [syngas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/syngas "Learn more about syngas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which is a mixture of H 2, CO, and minor amounts of other gaseous constituents. The syngas then undergoes the water-gas shift reaction to convert CO and H 2 O to H 2 and CO 2, producing an H 2 and CO 2-rich [gas mixture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/gas-mixture "Learn more about gas mixture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Speight et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0094)). The CO 2 can then be separated from H 2, using various technologies such as absorption, adsorption, or membranes, and the pure H 2 can be combusted with air in the power plant. Pre-combustion capture technology can use biomass and natural gas as [feedstocks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/feedstock "Learn more about feedstocks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Olabi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0069)).

The objective of this process is to convert the carbon fuel to carbon-less fuel: the [chemical energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/chemical-energy "Learn more about chemical energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of carbon is transformed into the chemical energy of hydrogen ([Kudapa et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772656824000502#bib0050)). A key advantage of pre-combustion capture over post-combustion capture is the higher CO 2 concentration and pressure achieved in the output stream. Abstract
--------

This review provides a comprehensive examination of Carbon Capture, Utilization, and Storage (CCUS) technologies, focusing on their advancements, challenges, and future prospects. It begins with an overview of carbon capture methods, including pre-combustion, post-combustion, and oxy-fuel combustion techniques, highlighting recent technological improvements and associated challenges. The review then explores various carbon utilization strategies, such as chemical conversion, biological processes, and mineralization, discussing emerging technologies, potential applications, and the economic and environmental benefits of utilizing captured CO 2. The discussion on carbon storage covers geological options like saline aquifers and depleted oil and gas fields, as well as recent advancements in monitoring and safety measures. The review assesses long-term stability and the risks linked to CO2 storage. In addressing the integration and optimization of CCUS systems, it evaluates the synergies between capture, utilization, and storage, includes techno-economic analyses of integrated systems, and presents case studies of successful CCUS projects. Economic feasibility and scalability are scrutinized through cost analysis, barriers to large-scale implementation, and the role of policy and regulatory frameworks. The review also identifies key research gaps, explores innovation potential, and provides strategic recommendations for advancing CCUS adoption. In conclusion, the findings underscore the essential role of CCUS in mitigating climate change, while highlighting the need for continued innovation, policy support, and cost reductions to enable its widespread deployment and effectiveness.

1. Introduction
---------------

The acceleration of climate change has escalated the need for drastic measures to reduce carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions ([Saravanan et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib206); [Saravanan and Kumar, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib205)). Global warming, driven primarily by anthropogenic CO 2 from industrial processes and energy production, has caused widespread environmental degradation ([Dubey and Arora, 2022a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib65)). The pressing challenge is to limit global temperature rise to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels, as outlined in international climate agreements ([Alli et al., 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib14), [2023b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib15), [2023c](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib16), [2023d](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib17)). However, the continuous reliance on fossil fuels makes achieving this goal daunting. Current mitigation strategies need to address the substantial volumes of CO 2 released into the atmosphere, particularly from heavy industries and power generation. The situation necessitates immediate and scalable solutions to prevent further environmental damage and ensure a transition towards a sustainable, low-carbon future ([Saravanan et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib207)).

Carbon Capture, Utilization, and Storage (CCUS) technologies have emerged as critical components in the effort to reduce CO2 emissions. These technologies are designed to capture CO2 directly from emission sources, such as power plants and industrial facilities, preventing its release into the atmosphere ([Yaashikaa et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib261)). Captured CO 2 can either be stored in deep geological formations or utilized in various applications such as chemical production or enhanced oil recovery. Recent advancements in CCUS technology, particularly in the areas of advanced sorbents, high-performance membranes, and electrochemical techniques, have significantly improved capture efficiency and cost-effectiveness ([Ketabchi et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib131); [Soo et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib227)). Furthermore, the utilization of captured CO 2 to produce valuable products, such as fuels and chemicals, offers an additional incentive for industries to adopt CCUS ([Alli et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib19)). Despite these advancements, significant challenges remain, especially concerning the long-term stability of CO 2 storage and the high costs associated with these technologies.

This review addresses the gap in the current research by focusing on a comprehensive evaluation of how advancements in capture, utilization, and storage technologies can be integrated to form an optimized CCUS system. While past studies have often evaluated these components in isolation, this work proposes a holistic approach, analyzing the interactions between the components to assess overall system effectiveness. We explore novel materials and methodologies that offer higher capture efficiencies and lower costs, such as next-generation sorbents and innovative electrochemical capture techniques. Moreover, the review delves into emerging CO2 utilization pathways that convert captured CO2 into economically valuable products, addressing both the environmental and economic challenges. The work also highlights advanced storage techniques, including recent developments in geological storage integrity and monitoring technologies that ensure long-term containment of CO2 with minimal risk of leakage.

The novelty of this study lies in its integrated evaluation of CCUS technologies, a crucial step that has been largely overlooked in previous research. While there have been significant individual advancements in CO 2 capture, utilization, and storage, this work uniquely examines how these components interact to create a cohesive, optimized CCUS strategy. Additionally, the review emphasizes how cutting-edge innovations in each component can be harmonized to enhance the overall performance and scalability of CCUS technologies. The primary objective of this work is to provide a detailed assessment of these advancements and propose solutions to optimize the integration of CCUS systems. By addressing these issues, this review aims to contribute to the development of more efficient and economically viable CCUS systems that can significantly mitigate CO 2 emissions, playing a vital role in combating climate change and meeting international climate targets.

2. Overview of carbon capture technologies
------------------------------------------

Researching carbon capture technology is crucial since it's thought to be the only way to reduce emissions from large-scale power plants, with the potential to cut emissions by 50% by the year 2050 ([Wei et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib248); [Wienchol et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib250)). It is noteworthy that if carbon capture and storage technologies are not taken into consideration, the cost of lowering emissions will increase drastically by 140% ([Kearns et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib129)). Carbon capture uses three basic technologies: pre-combustion, post-combustion, and oxyfuel combustion methods.

### 2.1. Pre-combustion carbon capture

The goal of pre-combustion capture is to remove CO 2 prior to fossil fuels burning ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#fig1)) ([Hua et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib111)). Prior to burning, fossil fuels are gasified and reconstituted by air and water vapor, resulting in CO 2 and H 2. [Chen and Chen (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib45) detailed that pre-combustion capture involves a steam-reforming process that yields CO and H 2 as well as a water-gas shift reaction that turns CO into CO 2. The CO 2 and H 2 produced by the process are separated using the gas separation technique, with CO 2 recovered and H 2 utilized as fuel for hydrogen gas generators ([Hua et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib111)) (see [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#fig2)).

By interacting with air or oxygen and with or without steam, conventional fuels (coal or natural gas) in this decarbonization process primarily create synthesis gas, which is a blend of carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H 2), commonly referred to as fuel gas or syngas ([Osman et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib186)). Equations [(1)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#fd1), [(2)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#fd2), which represent the partial oxidation and steam reforming reactions, respectively, represent the two primary syngas production processes ([Kheirinik et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib132)).(1)CnHm **+ (n/2)**O 2**→ nCO + (m/2)** H 2**ΔH**CH4**= −36 kJ mol**−1(2)**C****n****H****m**+**nH****2****O → nCO + (n+m/2) H****2****ΔH****CH4****= 206 kJ mol****−1**

The resulting mixture known as synthesis gas, or syngas, contains a smaller quantity of methane and other gaseous substances along with hydrogen and carbon monoxide ([Kheirinik et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib132)). After that, the syngas can go through the water-gas shift process, which turns CO and water (H 2 O) into H 2 and CO 2, creating a gas mixture that is rich in H 2 and CO 2 ([Osman et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib186)). The content of CO 2 in this combination might range between 15 and 50%. The CO 2 can then be caught, divided, conveyed, and eventually locked up, with the H 2-rich fuel combusted ([Wang and Han, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib243)). This is commonly used in Integrated Gasification Combined Cycle (IGCC) power plants.

As opposed to post-combustion technology, which extracts low-concentration CO 2 (between 5 and 15 percent), the shifted synthesis gas stream is higher-pressured and richer in CO 2, making it easier to get rid of before the H 2 burns ([Sarafraz and Christo, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib204)). Pre-combustion capture is often more effective due to the higher concentration of CO 2, although the capital expenditures of the base gasification procedure are frequently higher than those of ordinary pulverized coal power plants ([Kheirinik et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib132)). Furthermore, it allows the use of hydrogen, a clean fuel. However, the procedure is difficult, necessitating considerable changes to existing power plants as well as the development of new infrastructure ([Zhao et al., 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib276)). Physical or chemical absorption procedures can be used to separate the H 2 and CO 2 combination in the pre-combustion process by syngas scrubbing with a liquid solvent that is selective to carbon dioxide and hydrogen sulphide as acid compounds ([Kheirinik et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib132)). The most often used chemical solvent is amine-based, and compared to physical solvents, which have a higher partial pressure need, it has a greater absorption capacity at lower partial pressure ([Osman et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib186)). In contrast, Henry's law states that the loading of physical solvents is determined by the partial pressure of CO 2. Physical solvents operate best at low temperatures and high partial pressures because they have a higher sorption capacity ([Osman et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib186)). According to [Ashkanani et al. (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib23), physical solvents have certain disadvantages, including low CO 2 - H 2 specificity, high solvent density, heat stability, corrosivity, toxic effects, and flammability.

### 2.2. Post-combustion carbon capture

Post-combustion carbon capture is a technology that uses physical or chemical adsorption/absorption techniques to extract CO 2 and other gases from burning fossil fuel resources ([Chao et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib41)). These categories—adsorption, absorption, membrane separation, and chemical reactions—are based on the fundamentals of the capture procedure.

The post-combustion approach involves capturing and separating dilute CO 2 from flue gas in an oxidant setting. Before CO 2 collection, exhaust flue gas emissions undergo denitrification, desulphurization, dust elimination, and chilling to avoid the breakdown of solvents ([Wu et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib255)). The flue gas, primarily composed of CO 2, H 2 O, and N 2, is supplied counter-currently to an absorber holding a solvent, as seen in [Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#fig3).

The scrubbed gas is then cleaned with water, and CO 2 is regenerated ([Zhang et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib271)). Afterwards, the CO 2 undergoes compression into supercritical fluid and sent to saline aquifers or geological reserves for retention. Solvent regeneration necessitates an energy-intensive procedure due to the high flow rate and low concentration of CO 2 in flue gas streams, as well as its natural stability (Zhang et al., 2020).

In the post-combustion method, as stated by the research of [Zhang et al. (2024)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib275), monoethanolamine absorption appears to be the most popular and exclusive commercialized technique; other absorbents, like N-methyldiethanolamine and 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol, are also employed. Adsorption is also employed in post-combustion processes such as temperature swings, pressure swings, vacuum swings, electric swings, and hybrids ([Chao et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib41)).

Currently, chemical absorption techniques utilizing solvents derived from monoethanolamine (MEA) are employed in all commercial post-combustion CO 2 capture facilities ([Peu et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib191)). MEA was created more than 70 years ago as a general-purpose, non-selective solvent for clearing natural gas streams of acid gasses like CO 2 and hydrogen sulphide. When CO 2 is captured from flue gas, the method has been changed to include inhibitors that prevent solvent degradation and equipment corrosion ([Ooi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib185)). Aside from keeping the solvent strength (usually 20–30% amines by weight in water) low due to worries concerning corrosion and degradation, this also led to quite large equipment designs and high solvent regeneration costs.

The primary benefit of post-combustion capture is in its flexibility to be applied to current power plants, enabling retrofitting without significant changes ([Kheirinik et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib132)). Nonetheless, a major obstacle is the low concentration of CO 2 in flue gas (usually between 10 and 15 percent), which necessitates huge amounts of solvent and considerable energy input for renewal ([Raganati and Ammendola, 2024a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib196)). Furthermore, the process may become more complex due to solvent degradation and contaminants in flue gas.

### 2.3. Oxy-fuel combustion

The oxy-fuel combustion CC involves burning fossil fuels in pure oxygen, resulting in nitrogen-free flue gas generation of only CO 2 and H 2 O ([Wilberforce et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib253)). Flue gas condensation results in the production of a pure CO 2 stream and the removal of NOx pollutants. Due to the costly nature of producing and separating O 2, the carbon-based fuel's likelihood of commercialization is limited when using the oxyfuel combustion approach because it burns in a stream of pure oxygen (O 2) and recirculated flue gas instead of air ([Podder et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib192)). Wienchol and co-workers ([Wienchol et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib251)) posit it that although the process of capturing and separating CO 2 is straightforward, and the oxyfuel combustion method—which has a low efficiency penalty of 4% as opposed to 8–12% for the post-combustion route is thought to be the most feasible and energy-efficient path out of the three main methods (pre-, post-, and oxyfuel).

By using the oxyfuel combustion method in power systems, it is possible to reduce the amount of exhausted flue gas and nitrogen gas emissions (NOx) while also enhancing boiler efficiency ([Podder et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib192)). One major problem in such a route is the provision of pure oxygen, which is separated in the air separation unit using an energy-intensive and costly procedure. According to (Garcí et al., 2022), cryogenic distillation is the only technology that has been shown to be effective in creating huge quantities of highly pure O 2 for use on a wide scale. Therefore, it is crucial to look at potential new avenues for air separation, such as chemical looping techniques and membranes that transport oxygen and ions ([De Vos et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib59)). An important benefit of the oxyfuel combustion pathway is that it may be used in both new and existing power plants, and it can also be used with different kinds of fuels, such lignocellulosic biomass or municipal solid waste ([Wilberforce et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib253)).

### 2.4. Recent advancements in capture technologies

Rapid technological advancements and global population expansion have resulted in a large increase in fossil fuel usage, which is limited in supply and has an adverse effect on the environment ([Elsaid et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib76); [Abdelkareem et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib1)). The process of CCS entails separating carbon dioxide (CO 2) produced in the transportation and industrial sectors and storing it in a controlled area. According to ([Shen et al., 2022a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib213)), CCS is regarded as one of the realistic strategies that can be used to lessen global warming and its effects on humans and other living things.

The adoption of CCS has a direct effect on the cost of energy generation as well as the speed with which the technology may be adopted. Given the world's continued reliance on fossil fuels, it is imperative to develop a practical strategy for sequestering carbon dioxide from power plants ([Gür, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib102)) one of the main sources of CO 2 emissions. The process of capturing carbon dioxide (CO 2), which can be used in big power plants, entails compressing the gas and moving it to be stored in vast spaces like the oceans ([Humayun and Anwar, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666845924002010#bib115)).

Carbon Capture Utilisation and Storage
======================================

1.   [Energy system](https://www.iea.org/energy-system)
2.   [Carbon Capture Utilisation and Storage](https://www.iea.org/energy-system/carbon-capture-utilisation-and-storage)

[Overview](https://www.iea.org/energy-system/carbon-capture-utilisation-and-storage#overview)[How does CCUS work?](https://www.iea.org/energy-system/carbon-capture-utilisation-and-storage#how-does-ccus-work)[Tracking](https://www.iea.org/energy-system/carbon-capture-utilisation-and-storage#tracking)[Programmes](https://www.iea.org/energy-system/carbon-capture-utilisation-and-storage#programmes)

### What is carbon capture, utilisation and storage (CCUS)?

CCUS involves the capture of CO2, generally from large point sources like power generation or industrial facilities that use either fossil fuels or biomass as fuel. If not being used on-site, the captured CO2 is compressed and transported by pipeline, ship, rail or truck to be used in a range of applications, or injected into deep geological formations such as depleted oil and gas reservoirs or saline aquifers.

### What is the role of CCUS in clean energy transitions?

CCUS can be retrofitted to existing power and industrial plants, allowing for their continued operation. It can tackle emissions in hard-to-abate sectors, particularly heavy industries like cement, steel or chemicals. CCUS is an enabler of least-cost low-carbon hydrogen production, which can support the decarbonisation of other parts of the energy system, such as industry, trucks and ships. Finally, CCUS can remove CO2 from the air to balance emissions that are unavoidable or technically difficult to abate.

### Where do we need to go?

CCUS deployment has been behind expectations in the past but momentum has grown substantially in recent years, with over 700 projects in various stages of development across the CCUS value chain. Nevertheless, even at such level, CCUS deployment would remain well below what is required in the Net Zero Scenario.

Tracking Carbon Capture, Utilisation and Storage
------------------------------------------------

Not on track

Around 45 commercial facilities are already in operation applying carbon capture, utilisation and storage (CCUS) to industrial processes, fuel transformation and power generation. CCUS deployment has trailed behind expectations in the past, but momentum has grown substantially in recent years, with over 700 projects in various stages of development across the CCUS value chain. In 2023, announced capture capacity for 2030 increased by 35%, while announced storage capacity rose by 70%. This brings the total amount of CO2 that could be captured in 2030 to around 435 million tonnes (Mt) per year and announced storage capacity to around 615 Mt of CO2 per year. While this momentum from announcements is positive, it still just around 40% (and 60%, respectively) of the circa 1 Gt CO2 per year which is captured and stored in the Net Zero Emissions by 2050 (NZE) Scenario.

[Tracking Clean Energy Progress 2023](https://www.iea.org/reports/tracking-clean-energy-progress-2023)

### Country and regional highlights

The United States and Europe issue billions in funding for projects, while Germany and Japan make significant progress on CCUS legislation

The United States and Europe issue billions in funding for projects, while Germany and Japan make significant progress on CCUS legislation

Countries and regions making notable progress in CCUS include:

*   The **United States**announced important opportunities in 2023 that are expected to boost CCUS project development, including USD 1.7 billion for carbon capture demonstration projects and USD 1.2 billion for direct air capture (DAC) hubs under the 2021 [Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act](https://www.iea.org/policies/14982-infrastructure-and-jobs-act-carbon-capture-utilization-and-storage-investment).
*   The **European Union** issued around USD 1.5 billion to CCUS projects under the latest [Innovation Fund](https://climate.ec.europa.eu/eu-action/eu-funding-climate-action/innovation-fund/calls-proposals/large-scale-calls_en) round, and over USD 500 million to CO 2 transport and storage projects under its [Connecting Europe Facility](https://cinea.ec.europa.eu/programmes/connecting-europe-facility/energy-infrastructure-connecting-europe-facility-0_en) programme. Other notable funding for CCUS projects occurred in the **Netherlands** (USD 7.3 billion) and **Demark** (USD 1.2 billion). This is boosting project development, including first Dutch transport and storage project Porthos [reaching a final investment decision](https://www.porthosco2.nl/en/first-co2-storage-project-in-the-netherlands-is-launched/) (FID) to start injecting 2.5 Mt CO 2 per year in offshore gas fields in 2027, while injection for the first phase (25 kt CO 2 per year) of the [Ravenna CCS hub](https://www.eni.com/ravenna-ccs/en-IT/project/ravenna-hub.html) in Italy is set to start in 2024. Additionally, FIDs were reached for five capture facilities across application including in [ammonia](https://www.yara.com/corporate-releases/yara-invests-in-ccs-in-sluiskil-and-signs-binding-co2-transport-and-storage-agreement-with-northern-lights--the-worlds-first-cross-border-ccs-agreement-in-operation2/) and for two [hydrogen](https://www.airproducts.com/company/news-center/2023/11/1106-air-products-blue-hydrogen-plant-europe-and-exxonmobil-long-term-agreement) facilities in the [Netherlands](https://www.airliquide.com/sites/airliquide.com/files/2023-12/air-liquide-to-build-a-world-scale-co2-capture-unit-to-contribute-to-rotterdams-industrial-basin-decarbonization.pdf), and in [bioenergy with CCUS](https://orsted.com/en/media/news/2023/12/oersted-begins-construction-of-denmarks-first-carb-13757543) in Denmark.
*   **Germany** made its re-entry into CCUS with the release of its carbon management strategy that identifies CCUS as a crucial to meeting the country’s carbon neutrality ambitions by 2045. In addition to the strategy, the government plans to amend legislation to allow for the regulation of CCUS activities, including offshore CO 2 storage.
*   **Japan** is quickly advancing its CCUS efforts with the [selection of seven large-scale projects](https://www.meti.go.jp/english/press/2023/0613_001.html) to capture and store around 13 Mt CO 2 per year in 2030. It is also progressing on its draft CCS Business Act, which will establish a legal framework for CCUS across the value chain, and proposal to amend the London Protocol to allow for the transboundary storage of CO 2.

### CO2 capture

CCUS facilities currently capture more than 50 Mt CO2 annually

CCUS facilities currently capture more than 50 Mt CO2 annually

There are now around 45 commercial capture facilities in operation globally, with a total annual capture capacity of more than 50 Mt CO 2. Close to ten large-scale (capture capacity over 100 000 tCO 2/year, and over 1 000 tCO 2/yr for DAC applications) capture facilities entered operation in 2023, including the [Blue Flint ethanol project](https://ethanolproducer.com/articles/harvestone-co2-injection-commences-at-blue-flint-ethanol-ccs-project), [Linde Clear Lake capture facility](https://www.linde.com/news-and-media/2024/linde-starts-up-supply-of-clean-hydrogen-and-captured-carbon-dioxide-to-celanese), and [Heirloom](https://www.heirloomcarbon.com/news/heirloom-unveils-americas-first-commercial-direct-air-capture-facility) and [Global thermostat](https://www.globalthermostat.com/news-and-updates/global-thermostat-colorado-headquarters)’s first 1,000 tCO 2/yr facilities in the United States, and four projects in China (the [Jiling Petrochemical CCUS facility](http://www.tanjiaoyi.com/article-44241-1.html), the [CNOOC Enping oil field](https://www.cnoocltd.com/art/2023/6/1/art_55170_15339040.html), the first phase of the [Guanghui Energy CCUS integration project](https://news.bjx.com.cn/html/20230828/1328476.shtml) and the [China Energy Taizhou power plant](https://www.reuters.com/business/energy/china-energy-starts-operations-asias-largest-coal-carbon-capture-facility-2023-06-02/)). It also includes the [capture facility at the Petra Nova plant](https://www.reuters.com/business/energy/carbon-capture-project-back-texas-coal-plant-after-3-year-shutdown-2023-09-14/) in the United States, which restarted operations after a 3-year suspension. While planned capacity for 2030 increased by around 2030 since last year, the pipeline of current projects is only just around 40% of the Net Zero Scenario requirement in 2030.

Currently, around 65% of operating CO 2 capture capacity is at natural gas processing plants, one of the lowest-cost CO 2 capture applications, but the widespread adoption of economy-wide decarbonisation targets for 2050 is stimulating the diversification of CO 2 capture applications towards sectors that are key to reaching net zero. These include hard-to-abate industries, the power sector, the production of low-emissions hydrogen and ammonia, and atmospheric carbon dioxide removal. Based on the current project pipeline, by 2030 annual capture capacity from both new construction and retrofits could amount to around 95 Mt CO 2 from hydrogen production, around 90 Mt CO 2 from power generation, around 50 Mt CO 2 from industrial facilities (e.g. cement and steel production), and around 65 Mt CO 2 from DAC plant
